An Officer in a Superior Grade who has attained the age of fifty-five years may be required to retire, unless the Local Government considers him efficient, and permits him to remain in the Service. But, as the premature retirement of an efficient Officer imposes a needless charge on the State, this Rule should be worked with discretion.

2. The Accountant General will inform the Local Government whenever any Officer attains the age of fifty-five years, in order that the Local Government, being apprised of the fact, may take such action as it sees fit for the retention or otherwise of the Officer in question.

4. If an Officer, whose age is less than sixty years, is required to retire under Rule I., the head of his Office must certify the cause of the applicant's inefficiency. If the Officer wishes to retire of his own accord under Rule 5, the fact should be stated.

5. An Officer in Superior Service, who has attained the age of fifty-five years, may, at his option, retire from the Service on a Superannuation Pension.

6. After the age of fifty-five years, no Military or Naval Officer shall be appointed to a new office in Civil Employment, or shall be permitted to retain any such office which he has held for five years and upwards, except in special cases, which are to be referred for the decision of the Secretary of State. A Brigade Surgeon, or Surgeon Major, who entered the service on or after the 13th January, 1860, ceases to be in civil employment on attaining the age of 55 years.

7. Rule 6 should be strictly enforced unless the retirement of any officer should be considered by the Government under which he is serving to be injurious to the public interest.

RETIRING PENSIONS.

107. A Retiring Pension is granted to an Officer who voluntarily retires after completing the requisite period of service.

AMOUNT OF PENSION.

108. The amount of Pension that may be granted is determined by length of service, as set forth in Sections 109, 110, 111, and 112.

(1) An Officer entitled to Pension may not take a Gratuity instead of Pension.

FOR SUPERIOR SERVICE.

109. Compensation and Invalid Pension may be granted for Superior Qualifying Service as follows:—

(a) After a service of less than fifteen years:—

A Gratuity not exceeding (except in special cases and under the orders of the Government of India) One Month's Emoluments for each completed year of service or twelve months' Emoluments in all. If the Emoluments of the Officer have been reduced during the last five years of his service, otherwise than as a penalty, Average Emoluments may, at the discretion of the Local Government, be substituted for Emoluments.

(b) After a service of not less than fifteen years, but less than twenty-five years:—

A Pension not exceeding one-third of Average Emoluments; and also, not exceeding Rs. 3,000 a year, or, if the Average Emoluments do not exceed Rs. 12,000 a year, Rs. 2,000 a year.

(c) After a service of not less than twenty-five years:—

A Pension not exceeding one-half of Average Emoluments; and also, not exceeding Rs. 5,000 a year, or, if the Average Emoluments do not exceed Rs. 12,000 a year, Rs. 4,000 a year.

[Note.—The Proviso under Section 111 applies to an Officer invalided on account of incapacity for further service, which incapacity is due to old age or natural decay from advancing years. In the case of an Officer whose Qualifying Service began after he attained the age of twenty-five years, the attention of the Examining Medical Officer should always be called to that Proviso, in order that he may adapt his Certificate accordingly.]

110. An Invalid Pension on the following Scale, but not being less than Rs. 1,000 or more than Rs. 2,000 a year, may be granted to an Officer belonging to any of Classes described in Schedule B, except Class III. The Government of India may, on special grounds, recommend for sanction by the Secretary of State the grant of an Invalid Pension on the same scale to an Officer belonging to Class III., provided that if he was not appointed by the Secretary of State, he be not of purely Asiatic descent.

If the qualifying service of the officer be not less than— Forty-fifth parts of the officer's average emoluments.
10 years 10
11 " 11
12 " 12
13 " 13
14 " 14

111. The amount of Superannuation Pension which may be granted for Superior Qualifying Service is that which would be admissible as an Invalid Pension.

Proviso.—If an Officer's Qualifying Service began after the 20th January, 1871, and after he attained the age of 25 years, the pension admissible under this Section is to be multiplied by a fraction, the denominator of which is 30 or, for Officers entitled to the benefit of Section 114 (a), 27, and the numerator the number of years' service completed, not exceeding 30, or, for Officers entitled to the benefits of Section 114 (a), 27. The limit of Rs. 2,000, Rs. 3,000, Rs. 4,000, or Rs. 5,000 is to be applied before, and not after, this multiplication.

112. A Retiring Pension may be granted for Superior Qualifying Service as follows:—

After a service of not less than thirty years:

The same in amount as an Invalid Pension admissible after twenty-five years' service.

113. In the following cases the several prescribed maximum limits of Rs. 2,000, Rs. 3,000, Rs. 4,000, and Rs. 5,000 a year may be relaxed.

(a) For an Officer whose Average Emoluments exceed Rs. 10,000 a year, and who entered the Service before the 19th May, 1855, or whose salary before the 6th August, 1852, exceeded Rs. 10,000 a year, the maximum pension is ordinarily Rs. 5,000 a year. But if the service of such an Officer has been of extraordinary merit, a pension exceeding Rs. 5,000 a year may be recommended for the sanction of the Secretary of State.

(b) To an Officer who entered the Service before the 19th May, 1855, but whose Average Emoluments do not exceed Rs. 10,000 a year, the Secretary of State, on the recommendation of the Government of India, sometimes grants a Special Pension in excess of the prescribed maximum, for unusually meritorious services.

(c) For a Native Judge who was in the service on the 29th October, 1866, the limit is ordinarily Rs. 5,000 a year.

[Note.—The limits which may be relaxed under the foregoing Rules are the maximum money limits, and not the limits of one-third or one-half Average Emoluments.]

CHAPTER IX—continued.

114. In the following cases, certain privileges exist in respect of the length of service required for pension—

(a) To an Officer, not being a native of Asia, whose qualifying service began after twenty-five years of age, and whose whole service for which pension is claimed has been passed in one or other of the following offices, pension is admissible after three years' less service than is required from other Officers:—

Directors of Public Instruction.

Inspectors of Schools.

Principals and Professors of Colleges.

Head-masters of Colleges and High Schools.

Barristers serving in Offices other than those mentioned in Section 115 to which the Local Government has, with the consent of the Government of India, declared it to be necessary to appoint Barristers.

[Note.—The only office concerning which this declaration has been made is that of Deputy Superintendent and Remembrancer of Legal Affairs, Bengal.]

(c) The Government of India may, in special cases, add a period, not exceeding three years, to the service of any Civil Engineer appointed to the Public Works Department before the 1st November, 1873, with initial rank higher than that of a First Grade Assistant Engineer. This concession will not, however, be granted to any Officer taken over, directly or indirectly, from one of the Guaranteed Railway Companies, who received a bonus on leaving the service of the Company.

115. To the following officers, being Barristers and not being Covenanted Civil Servants, viz. :—

Judges of the Chief Court of the Punjab;

First Judges of Small Cause Courts of Presidency towns;

Recorder of Rangūn;

Secretary to the Government of India in the Legislative Department.

Special Pensions are admissible as follows:—

(a) After not less than six years and nine months' Active Service in one or other of the Offices specified in this Section,—an Invalid Pension of £300 a year.

(b) After not less than eight years and eight months' such service,—an Invalid Pension of £500 a year.

(c) After not less than eleven years and six months' such service,—a Retiring Pension of £750 a year.

1. Active Service here includes, besides time spent

on duty, whether in substantive or acting service, Privilege Leave, Subsidiary Leave, and periods of vacation during which the Officer is not on Furlough or Extraordinary Leave.

2. Except as provided by this Section, these Officers come under the ordinary Rules.

160. The widow of an Uncovenanted Officer, who receives a pension on account of her husband having been killed in the Mutiny, retains half her pension on re-marriage. Her half pension is not increased on second widowhood.

163. A pension is payable in India monthly on the first day of the following month.

(5) At the Home Treasury pensions are paid quarterly.

167. A pension is payable as follows:—

I. If it is stated in Rupees—

(a) At any Treasury of the Government of India in India; or,

(b) If the Pensioner is resident elsewhere than in Asia, at his option at the Home Treasury of the Government of India, at the rate of exchange fixed yearly for the adjustment of financial transactions between the Imperial and Indian Governments.

II. If it is stated in Sterling—

(a) At the Home Treasury of the Government of India; or,

(b) If the Pensioner is resident in Asia, at his option at any treasury of the Government of India in India, at the rate of exchange fixed yearly for the adjustment of financial transactions between the Imperial and Indian Governments.

1. The Rule for the payment at the Home Treasury of a pension at the rate of exchange fixed yearly for the adjustment of financial transactions between the Imperial and Indian Governments, applies to an Officer under covenant, who is entitled by his covenant to pension in accordance with the Rules applicable to Uncovenanted Officers; the covenanted rate of exchange for his pay and allowances does not, unless it is expressly so stated, apply to his pension.

169. Transfer of a Pension from the Home to an Indian Treasury is permitted whenever desired; but transfer of a Pension from an Indian to the Home Treasury is permitted only once.

SCHEDULE B.OFFICERS WHO ARE ENTITLED TO CERTAIN SPECIAL PRIVILEGES.

[SEE Sections 63, 64, 92, and 110.]

I. Officers of the Public Works Department trained in the Royal Indian Engineering College at Cooper's Hill.

II. "Stanley Engineers" of the Public Works Department, i.e., Civil Engineers appointed in England, in the years 1859 to 1871, under covenant and after competitive examination, in general accordance with Lord Stanley's despatch No. 11 (Public Works), dated 9th March, 1859.

III. Other Civil Engineers appointed to the Public Works Department—

(1) By the Secretary of State, before the 1st November, 1873—

(2) By competent authority in India—

(a) From the Civil Engineering Colleges in India:

(b) Others who are not of purely Asiatic descent.

IV. Officers of the Telegraph Department specially trained in Europe under Regulations made by the Secretary of State, including sixteen Officers who were specially trained in England to introduce the Morse system into India, and five Officers of the Indo-European Telegraph Department.

V. The following three Officers of the Agricultural Department, Madras, who are graduates of the Royal Agricultural College, or of some other institution of equal standing.

William Rowntree Robertson, Charles Benson, Kaspar Schiffmayer.

VI. Graded Officers of the Geological Survey Department.

VII. Officers of the Forest Department specially trained in Europe under Regulations made by the Secretary of State.

CHAPTER I.

EXTENT OF APPLICATION.

1. Saving as in any particular case specially so expressed, the Rules in this Code apply to all Public Officers as follows:—

The special Rules which apply to these Officers are contained in—

(a) Lieutenant-Governors and Ordinary Members of the Council of the Governor General or of the Governor of Madras or Bombay ...

Chapter III.

(b) Judges of the High Court and (provided that they are Barristers and not Covenantal Civil Servants) Judges of the Chief Court of the Punjab, the Secretary to the Government of India in the Legislative Department, the Recorder of Rangoon and First Judges of Small Cause Courts of Presidency Towns ...

IV.

(c) Covenantal Civil Servants, Military Officers subject to the Civil Rules, and Uncovenantal Officers belonging to one of the Classes enumerated in Schedule A, and appointed thereto by Her Majesty's Secretary of State, or with his particular sanction, or whose names are entered in Schedule B.

V.

(1) The Officers whose names are entered in Schedule B were admitted to the benefits of the Rules in Chapter V. from the 3rd January, 1872, being the day on which the despatch from the Secretary of State, No. 451, dated 6th December, 1871, was received. The death, resignation, removal, or retirement of any of these Officers should be promptly reported by the Government, Department or Head of Office

under whom he is serving, directly to the Government of India in the Department of Finance

2 The conditions precedent to the entry of the name of an Officer in Schedule B were that—

  • (1) he was not of purely Asiatic descent.
  • (2) he held substantively, on the 3rd January, 1872, one of the offices described in Schedule A; and
  • (3) his salary, on the 10th February, 1876, was not less than six thousand rupees a year.

3 An Uncovenantal Officer to whom the Rules in Chapter V are declared applicable, does not forfeit his privileges upon transfer to some office not included in Schedule A, unless such transfer is ordered as a penalty, or because of the Officer's inefficiency.

4 The Rules in Chapter V which apply to an Uncovenantal Officer apply to an Officer of the Royal Navy attached to the Marine Survey Department. The leave admissible to such an Officer will be calculated with reference only to his service in India, and without any regard to his British service.

(d) Native Civil Servants ... Chapter VI.

(e) Law Officers described in the pre-fixed note to Chapter VII, provided that they are not Covenantal Civil Servants ... VII.

(f) Bishops ... VIII.

(g) Chaplains ... IX.

(h) Uncovenantal Officers excepting those specified in case (c)... X.

(i) The Bengal Covenantal Pilot Service ... XI.

(k) The Police at Port Blair ... XII.

(m) Officers serving under Special Contracts ... Their contracts.

CHAPTER II.

GENERAL RULES.

3. The Government of India reserves to itself the full right of modifying the Rules in this Code as may from time to time seem to it expedient, and of interpreting these Rules in case of dispute. In all contracts for service in India to which the Secretary of State is a party, provision is made for the exercise by the Government of these rights.

4. Leave of Absence can never be claimed absolutely, as of right. Nothing in this Code must be understood to limit the free discretion of the Government to refuse or revoke Leave of Absence of any description, at any time, according to the exigencies of the Public Service.

1. If a Local Government, under this Section, refuse Furlough to a Member of the Bengal Covenantal Civil Service, it should report the refusal for the confirmation of the Government of India in the Home Department.

5. (a) An Officer recalled to duty before the expiry of leave of any kind is entitled to take the balance of his leave, together with any leave of the same kind subsequently earned, as soon as he can be spared from duty

[Note.—Time occupied in the voyage to India on recall from recognised leave out of India is not included in the balance of leave.]

(b) An Officer recalled to duty before the expiry of any recognised leave out of India is entitled to free passage to India, and actual personal travelling expenses from the port of debarkation in India to the Station to which he is posted;

[Note.—During the voyages to and from India Leave Allowances only are granted.]

(c) An Officer recalled to duty before the expiry of leave in India, other than Privilege Leave, is treated as on duty from the date on which he starts for the Station to which he is ordered, and is entitled to his actual personal travelling expenses on his journey to the Station to which he is posted.

(d) An Officer recalled to duty from Privilege Leave is entitled to no concession except that mentioned in Clause (a).

6. If an Officer while absent on Leave during which the amount of his Leave Allowances depends upon the substantive or officiating rank or appointment which he

CHAPTER II—continued.

would hold if present on duty, obtains promotion, substantive or officiating, or an increase of Pay or Acting Allowance which involves no change in, or addition to, his duties or responsibilities, the promotion or increase has effect at once. Otherwise the promotion of an absent Officer has effect only from the date of his return to duty.

7. An Officer who is discharged from, or resigns, the Public Service, and is re-employed after an interval, cannot, without the sanction of the Government of India, count his service before discharge towards leave after re-employment.

Exception.—An Officer discharged from the Indian Navy on its abolition, and subsequently re-employed, may count his Naval Service towards leave after re-employment, notwithstanding the occurrence of a moderate interval between his discharge from the Indian Navy and his first re-employment. Any leave taken by such Officer while in the Indian Navy, will be treated, in calculating the leave due to him under this Rule, as if such leave had been taken under the Rules to which he became subject on re-employment.

8. Service as Private Secretary to the Governor-General or a Governor qualifies, provided that the Officer belongs to the Covenantal Civil Service or to the Staff Corps, or belonged (before his appointment as Private Secretary) to the Uncovenantal Service.

13. Subject to the Provisions of Section 54, if an Officer makes over charge of his Office before noon, his leave begins on and includes the day on which he makes over charge; otherwise, it begins on and includes the following day.

1. If the absence of the Officer does not involve the immediate deputation of any Officer from another Station, or any formal transfer of charge in the way of taking over money, or the like, then the Absentee may leave his Station at the close of the last day on which the Office is open for business before his leave begins. In such case the several transfers of charge dependent upon the departure of the Absentee shall take effect from the close of the last working day as aforesaid. But, subject to the provisions of Section 54, the leave of the Absentee and the re-arrangement of allowances, if any, depending upon his departure, shall begin from, and include, the first day thereafter on which the Office is open for business. The actual date of the Officer's departure from his Station must, however, always be reported.

CHAPTER III.LIEUTENANT-GOVERNORS AND MEMBERS OF COUNCIL.

18. Leave on Medical Certificate for not more than six months may be granted to a Lieutenant-Governor. On resuming his duties after such Leave, a Lieutenant-Governor is entitled to half his salary for the period of his absence. If he is prevented from resuming his duties he is entitled to no Absentee Allowances. A Lieutenant-Governor is not entitled to any other Leave.

19. The leave admissible to an Ordinary Member of the Executive Council of the Governor-General, or of the Governor of Madras or Bombay, is regulated by Act of Parliament, Section 26 of 24 & 25 Vic., cap. 67 as follows:—

Section 26 of 24 & 25 Vic., cap. 67. It shall be lawful for the Governor-General in Council, or Governor in Council of either of the Presidencies, as the case may be, to grant to an Ordinary Member of Council, Leave of Absence, under Medical Certificate, for a period not exceeding six months, and such Member, during his absence, shall retain his Office, and shall, on his return and resumption of his duties, receive half his salary

for the period of such absence; but, if his absence shall exceed six months, his Office shall be vacated.

1. Subject to any special orders by the Government of India to the contrary,

Leave of absence granted to an ordinary Member of the Council of the Governor General (if taken out of India) commences on the day after such Member embarks at Bombay or Calcutta, and ends the day before he disembarks at either of these ports: Provided always that such Member has not been relieved of the charge of his office until he embarks, and that he resumes charge immediately upon his disembarkation:

And the tenure by a Member of Council of his office begins when he takes his seat in Council under the usual salute, and the resignation of his office by a Member of Council whose successor has not entered upon his office, takes effect from the day following that of his embarkation at Bombay or Calcutta, or from the expiry of his five years' tenure of office, whichever date is earlier.

CHAPTER IV.JUDGES OF HIGH COURTS AND CERTAIN LEGAL AND JUDICIAL OFFICERS

Regulations by the Secretary of State in Council of India under the provisions of 24 & 25 Victoria, Cap. 104, Section 6, respecting the furloughs * * * of the Chief Justices and Puisne Judges of the High Courts of Judicature, for the Bengal Division of the Presidency of Fort William, Madras, Bombay and the North-Western Provinces; and Regulations by the Secretary of State in Council respecting the furloughs and leaves of absence of certain other Legal and Judicial Officers.

DEFINITIONS.

20. In the following Rules "Actual Service" includes—

  1. (1) Time spent on duty as a Judge or Officiating Judge of a High Court.
  2. (2) Privilege Leave of Absence.
  3. (3) Subsidiary Leave of Absence.
  4. (4) Duly authorised Vacations, provided that the Judge is not absent on Furlough or Extraordinary Leave.

"Extraordinary Leave" means any leave granted otherwise than under these Rules.

FURLOUGH AND SUBSIDIARY LEAVE.

21. One year's Furlough shall be placed to the credit of each Judge, after the completion of every four years of Actual Service.

22. Except under Sections 25 and 26 no furlough shall be granted until at credit under Section 21.

Provided that any Judge who may have been already in the service of the Government at the time of taking office in the High Court, and who was, at that time, entitled under the Rules applicable to the branch of the Service to which he belonged, to Furlough, without Medical Certificate, may be granted Furlough for a term not exceeding that to which he was so entitled, upon the condition that such Furlough shall not be taken until the completion of two years' Actual Service in the High Court, and shall not exceed one year.

23. Except under Section 26, Furlough shall not be repeated until after the completion of three years' Actual Service from the date of the last return from Furlough or Extraordinary Leave.

N.B. The words "Furlough," or "Extraordinary Leave," in the foregoing Section mean Furlough under these Rules, or Extraordinary Leave granted to any Officer since he has been as a Chief Justice or Judge of a High Court.

24. The maximum term of Furlough to be taken at any one time shall be fifteen months.

25. Under Medical Certificate, Furlough may be granted before it is at credit under Section 21.

26. On Urgent Private Affairs, Furlough may be granted before it is at credit under Section 21, and although three years of Actual Service have not been completed since the last return from Furlough or Extraordinary Leave.

Provided that Furlough under this Section shall not exceed six months, and shall be granted only once during the whole period of a Judge's service in the High Court.

27. The aggregate amount of all Furlough which can be granted to a Judge during the whole period of his service in the High Court is two years and a half.

28. Furlough taken in India shall be reckoned from the date of the Judge's quitting his Office to the date of his return thereto. Furlough taken out of India shall be reckoned from the date of embarkation to the date of debarkation.

In the event of the Furlough being taken partly in India and partly out of India, the commencement and termination of the Furlough shall be respectively de-

termined by the foregoing Rules, according as the Furlough begins or ends in or out of India.

29. For the interval elapsing between the date of quitting his Office and the commencement of Furlough out of India, and between the termination of Furlough out of India and resuming his Office, a Judge may be allowed Subsidiary Leave not ordinarily exceeding thirty days, which, in special cases, may be extended.

30. A Judge, when on Furlough or Subsidiary Leave, shall receive allowances at the rate of Rs. 833-5-4 per mensem in the case of Leave taken in India, or £1,000 per annum in the case of Leave taken out of India.

31. Except under Medical Certificate, the number of Furloughs to be granted at any one time, and the grant of Furlough to individual Judges shall be subject to, and limited by, the exigencies of the Service, which exigencies shall be determined exclusively by the Authority granting the Furlough.

32. If, owing to the exigencies of the Service, it may be necessary to place any limit on the number of Judges who may be absent on Furlough at the same time, applications not supported by Medical Certificate will be granted in the following order:

The Judge to whose credit the greatest amount of Furlough remains under Section 21 shall have the preference.

If two or more Applicants are on an equality in this respect, the preference shall be given to the applicant whose actual service in a High Court is longest, reckoning, in the case of a Judge who has not taken Furlough or Extraordinary Leave, from the date of his commencement of service in the High Court, and, in the case of a Judge who has taken Furlough or Extraordinary Leave, from the date of his last return from such Furlough or Leave.

If two or more Applicants are equal in both respects, the preference shall be given to the senior in the Court.

PRIVILEGE LEAVE.

33.—(a). Subject to the exigencies of the Public Service, a Judge, who has completed eleven months' continuous duty including the Vacation, may take Privilege Leave for one month in each year; but, except as provided in Clause (b), his salary will cease during such Leave. A Judge may not take Privilege Leave in instalments, or more than a month at once; and, except as provided in Clause (c), Privilege Leave may not be combined with the Vacation.

(b) A Judge who is detained on duty as Vacation-Judge, may, during his next Privilege Leave, draw his salary for any period by which his vacation, consequently, falls short of one month.

(c) Once in three years, and not oftener, Privilege Leave, during which full salary is drawn under Clause (b), may be prefixed or affixed to the Vacation.

34. Section 33 does not apply to those Judges (32 in number) who were in office on the date of the publication of that Rule.

35. Privilege Leave may be granted to any of the Officers referred to in Section 34, under the following Rules:—

(1) Subject to the exigencies of the Public Service, Privilege Leave for one month may be granted to a Judge who has been on duty, without interruption, for eleven months, including the Vacation.

(2) An additional month of Privilege Leave accrues to a Judge after each further period of 'eleven months' continuous duty including the Vacation.

CHAPTER IV—continued.

(3) Privilege Leave may not be taken in installments; but any Privilege Leave not taken when due may be added to the next Privilege Leave which accrues:

Provided that the whole Privilege Leave taken at one time shall not exceed three months, and that any accumulation of Privilege Leave beyond that period shall be forfeited.

(4) Saving as provided in Rule (5), a Judge, when on Privilege Leave, shall receive half his salary

(5) A Judge who discharges the duties of Vacation-Judge, during the whole or any part of a Vacation, shall be entitled, for each day of such duty, to draw his full salary for one day of any Privilege Leave which he may subsequently obtain.

GENERAL.

36. Furlough and Privilege Leave shall not be taken as such, in continuation of each other; but if a Judge absent on one of these classes of Leave be allowed to change it for another, the whole period of his absence shall be held to be under the class of Leave for which it was changed.

37. Applications for Leave shall in all cases be submitted in such manner as the Government may, from time to time, prescribe.

38. Leave Allowances shall be payable monthly if payment is taken in India, and quarterly if payment is taken in England.

39. No substantive appointment shall be vacated, merely by reason of Leave being granted under these Rules.

40. If a Judge shall overstay any Leave, he shall forfeit all salary during the time of his remaining so absent, and if he shall so continue absent for more than one week, his office shall be liable to become vacant. This Rule shall not operate to oblige any Judge

taking leave, other than leave under Section 33, to return to duty on an authorised holiday, unless an Officer is officiating as Judge in consequence of his absence.

41. No Leave, except Privilege Leave and Leave Subsidiary to Furlough, shall count as service for pension.

42. If a Judge, who is a Covenantal Civil Servant or a Native Civil Servant, shall be permitted to resign his office and remain in the service, all Leave which he may have taken as a Judge of the High Court shall be reckoned as if it had been taken under the Rules for the Leave of Absence of Covenantal Civil Servants, or Native Civil Servants, as the case may be.

43. The foregoing Rules regarding Furlough and Leave of absence (Sections 20 to 42), are, with the modifications A and B below stated, applicable also to the following Officers, provided that they were before their appointment practising Barristers of England or Ireland, or practising Members of the Faculty of Advocates of the Court of Session of Scotland.

Judges of the Chief Court of the Punjab.

Recorders in Rangoon.

First Judges of Small Cause Courts at Presidency Towns.

Secretary to the Government of India in the Legislative Department.

Modifications indicated above.

(A) The Allowances of these Officers, while on Furlough or Subsidiary Leave, shall not exceed half their salaries, and shall be limited also to the rate prescribed in Section 30.

(B) The Privilege Leave of the Secretary to the Council of the Governor-General for making Laws and Regulations, the First Judge of the Small Cause Court at Calcutta, and of the Judges of the Chief Court of the Punjab shall be regulated by Sections 70 to 75.

CHAPTER V.

COVENANTED CIVIL SERVANTS NOT BEING NATIVE CIVIL SERVANTS; MILITARY OFFICERS SUBJECT TO CIVIL RULES; AND UNCOVENANTED OFFICERS BELONGING TO ONE OF THE CLASSES ENUMERATED IN SCHEDULE A AND APPOINTED THERETO BY HER MAJESTY'S SECRETARY OF STATE, OR WITH HIS PARTICULAR SANCTION, OR WHOSE NAMES ARE ENTERED IN SCHEDULE B.

GENERAL.

44. No Leave but Privilege Leave may be granted to a Covenanted Civil Servant who has completed thirty-five years' service, or to a Military Officer in Civil Employ, whether subject to Civil Rules or to Military Rules, who is more than fifty-five years old. Any Leave other than Privilege Leave granted to a Covenanted Civil Servant before such date ceases to have effect on such date, and any Leave other than Privilege Leave granted in the Civil Department to a Military Officer in Civil Employ before he is fifty-five years old ceases to have effect on his fifty-fifth birthday, on which date he reverts to Military Employ. The absentee allowances of a Military Officer in Civil Employ on Leave other than Privilege Leave become a Military Charge, and he becomes subject to Military Rules on his fifty-fifth birthday, whether his Leave was granted in the Military or the Civil Department.

45. An Uncovenanted Officer is eligible, after he attains the age of fifty-five years, for Privilege Leave, and also for any Special Leave (Sections 60 and 129) to which he may be otherwise entitled, and for no other kind of Leave. Any Leave, other than Privilege Leave or Special Leave, granted to an Uncovenanted Officer before the date on which he attains the age of fifty-five years, ceases to have effect on this date.

FURLOUGH.

[Note.—The Local Government in the Civil Department may grant Furlough in India under Military Rules to a Military Officer subject to Military Rules who is entitled to Furlough under Chapter I, of the Rules published by G.G.O. in Military Department, No 171, dated 25th February, 1874. Such Furlough should be granted only upon a Certificate by the Government in the Military Department of the Presidency to which the Officer belongs, that he is entitled thereto; and a copy of the Order granting the Furlough should be sent to that Government.

A Local Government may not, in the Civil Department, grant Furlough out of India under Military Rules to any Military Officer subject to Military Rules.]

46. The amount of Furlough admissible to a Covenanted Civil Servant, or a Military Officer subject to Civil Rules and the aggregate amount of Furlough and of Special Leave With Allowances, admissible to an Uncovenanted Officer, is limited to six years. All the Rules in this Code are subject to this limitation.

Explanation.—The following Leave under other Rules is counted as Furlough under this Section.

In the case of a Covenanted Civil Servant:—Furlough and Leave with Allowances on Medical Certificate under former Rules.

  • (1) Leave of the kind described in Definition "Active Service" (h), and Junior Furlough under the Rules in force before the 8th June, 1855, are not counted against an Officer under this Section.

[Note.—Junior Furlough was Medical Leave, involving loss of appointment taken before completion of three years' residence.]

In the case of a Military Officer subject to Civil Rules:—Furlough under Rules 1 and 6 of the Military Furlough and Leave Rules of 1875.

In the case of an Uncovenanted Officer:—Furlough and Leave With Allowances on Medical Certificate, and Leave on Private Affairs under former Rules, or under the Rules in Chapter X.

47. The amount of Furlough "earned" by an Officer is one-fourth of his Active Service.

  • 1. Leave of the kind described in Definition "Active Service" (h) counts as Active Service under this Section.

Proviso.—Provided that, in making this calculation for an Uncovenanted Officer, only half of the Active service rendered by him in offices other than those included in Schedule A, shall be taken into account.

1. The whole of the Active Service rendered by the Officer while officiating in any of the offices included in Schedule A is to be taken into account.

48. The amount of Furlough "due" to an Officer is the amount which he has earned, diminished by—

In the case of a Covenanted Civil Servant:—

  • (1) The Furlough which he has enjoyed under these or any former Rules, except Junior Furlough under the Rules in force before the 8th June 1855;
  • (2) The excess over one year in the amount of Leave with Allowances, on Medical Certificate which he has enjoyed under former Rules.

In the case of a Military Officer subject to Civil Rules:—

  • (3) The Furlough which he has enjoyed under the Rules of this Code, or Rules 1 and 6 of the Military Furlough and Leave Rules of 1875.

In the case of an Uncovenanted Officer:—

  • (4) The Furlough, or Special Leave, with Allowances, which he has enjoyed under these or any other Rules;
  • 5) The Leave on Private Affairs or on Medical Certificate with Allowances, which he has enjoyed under the Rules formerly applicable to him.

Proviso.—Provided that if more than two years' Furlough be due to an Uncovenanted Officer when he first becomes subject to these Rules, the excess shall be cancelled.

49. To an Officer who has rendered three years' Continuous Service, Furlough for not more than two years may be granted, as follows:

On Medical Certificate.—unconditionally;

Without Medical Certificate.—subject to these provisions:—(1) That the Furlough be due to him; (2) that he have rendered eight years' Active Service; (3) that the whole number of Officers absent on Furlough and Special Leave do not exceed the limit (if any) appointed by the Government of India.

  • 1. Except on Medical Certificate, or on very Urgent Private Affairs, Furlough or Special Leave may not be granted to a Covenanted Civil Servant or a Military Officer subject to Civil Rules, in Oudh, the Central Provinces, British Burma, Assam, Ajmir, Coorg, and Berar, if one-fifth of all the Officers serving in the same Commission are already absent on Furlough or Special Leave. Elsewhere, it may not be granted, if one-fifth of the whole number of Covenanted Civil Servants or Military Officers subject to Civil Rules serving under the orders of the same Local Government are already absent on Furlough or Special Leave.

50. Furlough granted under Section 49 may, on Medical Certificate, be extended to not more than three years.

51. To an Officer who has not rendered three years' Continuous Service, Furlough for not more than one year may be granted on Medical Certificate.

52. Furlough granted under Section 51 for less than one year may, on Medical Certificate, be extended to not more than one year.

53. (a) Subject to his good conduct and efficiency, an Officer on Ordinary Furlough, has a Lien on his substantive appointment, or on a substantive appointment of a like character and not less pay.

Explanation. Ordinary Furlough includes —

(1) The first two years of each separate period of Furlough under Section 49, including any extension under Section 50;

1. Ordinary Furlough can, under no circumstances extend beyond two years at one time.

(2) So much of Furlough under Section 51, including any extension under Section 52, as may be due: Provided that the Officer have rendered six months' Continuous Active Service.

(b) On Other Furlough a Covenant Civil Servant, or a Military Officer subject to Civil Rules, has no Lien; but an Uncovenanted Officer has a Lien.

(3) *An Officer on Furlough has no claim to substantive promotion, but there is no objection to such promotion.

(5) An Officer cannot obtain Ordinary Furlough or retain allowances under Section 56, unless he has a substantive appointment. A Military Officer who has no substantive Civil appointment continues subject to Military Rules, and can only get Military Furlough Allowances.

54. †Saving as provided in Section 66. Furlough and Special Leave out of India begin on and include the day of the departure of the vessel in which the Officer sails, and end on and include the day before the arrival of the vessel in which the Officer returns.

1. †An Officer may leave India, or return to India, by any port; but his Furlough or Special Leave begins on the vessel's departure from the port where he first meets it, and ends on the day before its arrival at the port where he last quits it.

Example.—The Furlough or Special Leave of an Officer sailing from Calcutta in a vessel which touches at Madras, begins on the departure of the vessel from Calcutta, and not from Madras.

2. †The date of the departure of a vessel in charge of a pilot is the date on which the pilot leaves it at sea; and the date of the arrival of a vessel in charge of a pilot is the date on which the pilot is taken on board.

3. *†An Officer serving in Persia or Arabia may leave the country in which he is serving, or return to it, by any port or frontier town except Aden. The date of his departure or return must be certified by the British Consul, or, if there be no British Consul, by the Officer himself.

4. †An Officer taking Furlough or Special Leave out of India must report his embarkation to the Local Government which granted his leave, and his arrival in England to the Secretary of State.

55. An Officer is not entitled, at the end of Furlough or Special Leave, or Subsidiary Leave affixed thereto, to resume, as a matter of course, without further orders, the particular appointment which he vacated before his Furlough or Special Leave. He should report his return to duty in the manner prescribed in Section 162, Rule 6, and await orders.

* Applies to all Officers.

† Applies to a Military Officer subject to Military Rules.

‡ Applies to all Officers excepting Officers subject to Chapter X.

56. An Officer on Ordinary Furlough is entitled, subject to the limitations prescribed in Sections 57 and 58, to a Leave Allowance equal to half his Average Salary.

57. The Maximum Leave Allowance of a Covenant Civil Servant or a Military Officer subject to Civil Rules, on Ordinary Furlough is, if paid at the Home Treasury of the Government of India, £250 a quarter; if paid in India Rs. 833\frac{1}{3} a month. The minimum is, if paid at the Home Treasury, £125 a quarter; or his last salary, whichever is less; if paid in India, Rs. 416\frac{2}{3} a month, or his last salary, whichever is less.

58. The Maximum Leave Allowance of an Uncovenanted Officer on Ordinary Furlough is, if paid at the Home Treasury of the Government of India, £200 a quarter, or if paid in India, Rs. 666\frac{2}{3} a month. There is no minimum.

1. An Officer on Furlough does not forfeit his past Leave Allowances by resigning the Service without returning to India.

59. An Officer on Furlough other than Ordinary, is entitled, if a Covenant Civil Servant, or a Military Officer subject to Civil Rules, to Subsistence Allowance; if an Uncovenanted Officer, to £120 a quarter paid at the Home Treasury, or Rs. 400 a month paid in India, or to one quarter of his Average Salary, whichever is less.

1. The following are the rates of monthly Subsistence Allowance for a Covenant Civil Servant or a Military Officer subject to Civil Rules: —

To an Officer of not more than eight years' actual residence in India. Rs. 250
To an Officer of more than eight, but less than twelve years' actual residence in India ... .. „ 320
To an Officer of twelve or more years' actual residence in India ... .. „ 400

Note.—The Monthly Subsistence Allowance of a Native Civil Servant when out of employ is as follows: —

To an Officer who has rendered not more than eight years' Active Service ... .. Rs. 250
To an Officer who has rendered more than eight, but less than twelve years' Active Service ... .. „ 320
To an Officer who has rendered not less than twelve years' Active Service ... .. „ 400

SPECIAL LEAVE.

60. Special Leave on Urgent Private Affairs may be granted at any time for not more than six months:

Provided that an Officer who has had Special Leave must render six years' active service before he can again have such Leave.

(1) The corresponding Leave under the Covenant Civil Service Leave Rules in force before the 1st July 1868, is "Special Leave" within the meaning of this proviso.

(2) An Uncovenanted Officer is not debarred from obtaining Special Leave with allowances under this Chapter by reason of his having had Leave on Private Affairs under the Rules in Chapter X., which is Furlough in another form.

61. An Officer on Special Leave has a lien on his Substantive appointment, or on a Substantive appointment of a like character and not less pay, as if he were on Ordinary Furlough.

62. For the first six months for which an Officer is on Special Leave, whether the six months be included in the same Leave or not, he is entitled to the Leave Allowance admissible under Sections 56, 57, and 58. Thereafter he is entitled to no Leave Allowance.

(1) The corresponding Leave under the Covenant Civil Service Leave Rules in force before the 1st July, 1868, is "Special Leave" within the meaning of this Section.

SUBSIDIARY LEAVE.

63*.—(a) Subsidiary Leave is the time allowed—

  1. (1) To an Officer leaving India, on retiring from the Service, or on Furlough or Special Leave, to break up his domestic establishment and travel to the port of embarkation, and
  2. (2) To an Officer returning to India from Furlough or Special Leave, to travel from the port of debarkation and re-organise his domestic establishment.

Note.—The use of the words, "port of embarkation" and "port of debarkation" limits the grant of Subsidiary Leave to an Officer leaving India by sea, that is at an Indian sea-port. No Subsidiary Leave is admissible to an Officer who does not leave India by sea.

(b) The grant to an Officer leaving India—

  1. (1) Of permission to retire from the Service, or
  2. (2) Of Furlough or Special Leave out of India, carries with it the grant of Subsidiary Leave, including, in Case (2), Subsidiary Leave on his return to duty.

64 * The minimum Subsidiary Leave is ten days; otherwise Subsidiary Leave is calculated as Joining Time is calculated. (Section 157, Rule 2.)

Note.—All the provisos to Rule 2 under Section 157 apply equally in the case of Subsidiary Leave.

65.* If an Officer eventually fails to obtain the medical certificate required by Section 78. Rule 10 or 13, he is entitled to Joining Time from the date on which the decision of the Medical Board or Officer is communicated to him, to enable him to return to his appointment. During this Joining Time, his allowances are regulated as if he was on Subsidiary Leave.

66.* An Officer may take Furlough or Special Leave, partly in, and partly out, of India. But Subsidiary Leave is not admissible unless the Furlough or Special Leave begins, or, as the case may be, ends, as Furlough or Special Leave out of India.

Proviso.—If an Officer going on Furlough or Special Leave out of India, is prevented by sickness or other urgent and adequate reason not within his own control, such, for example, as the postponement of the departure of the vessel in which his passage is engaged, from embarking within his Subsidiary Leave, the Local Government may order that his Furlough or Special Leave shall begin in India at the end of his Subsidiary Leave, without forfeiture of his Subsidiary Leave.

  1. 1. *In the absence of such special order, if an Officer remains in India after the end of Subsidiary Leave, his Furlough or Special Leave dates from the beginning of his Subsidiary Leave.
  2. 2. †An Officer returning to India, without the permission of the authority which granted him Leave, more than fourteen days before the end of his Furlough or Special Leave, is not entitled to Subsidiary Leave, save under the special orders of the Local Government.

67. An Officer on Subsidiary Leave under Section 63 (a), (1), has, or has not, a lien on an appointment according as he has, or has not, such lien on the first day of the leave to which it is subsidiary.

Note.—A Military Officer subject to Military Rules does not lose his lien during Subsidiary Leave preparatory to Furlough.

68. An Officer on Subsidiary Leave under Section 63 (a), (2), has, or has not, a lien on an appointment according as he has, or has not, such lien on the day last preceding the Subsidiary Leave.

69. An Officer on Subsidiary Leave is entitled to allowances as follows:—

  1. (a) If the leave be Subsidiary to Special Leave, and the Officer have had leave subsidiary to a former Special Leave: no allowance.

(1) The corresponding Leave under the Covenantated Civil Service Rules in force before the 1st July, 1868, is Special Leave within the meaning of this Clause.

(b) If the leave be subsidiary to Ordinary Furlough or Special Leave:—the allowance admissible under Section 56, but calculated, in the case of a Covenantated Civil Servant, or a Military Officer subject to Civil Rules, without the limitations of Maximum or Minimum prescribed by Section 57. The limitations prescribed by Section 58, apply to the allowances of an Uncovenanted Officer on Subsidiary Leave.

(c) If the Leave be Subsidiary to Furlough other than Ordinary, the allowance admissible under Section 59.

(d) If the Officer, under Section 162, have ceased to have a lien on a substantive appointment—as, for example, if a Covenantated Officer has been absent on Extraordinary Leave in extension of Furlough—in the case of a Covenantated Civil Servant, or a Military Officer subject to Civil Rules:—Subsistence Allowance; and, in the case of an Uncovenanted Officer:—no allowance.

1. *An Officer may draw allowances as if he were on Privilege Leave for any part of his Subsidiary Leave under Section 63 (a), (1), for which, if he were not retiring from the Service, or going on Furlough or Special Leave, Privilege Leave would be admissible to him. It is to be observed that an Officer who has no lien on an appointment cannot benefit by this Rule (see Rule 3 under Section 75).

PRIVILEGE LEAVE.

[The Rules in this Chapter apply to Military Officers subject to Military Rules.]

70. The amount of Privilege Leave admissible at one time is limited to three calendar months.

71. The amount of Privilege Leave earned by an Officer is one-eleventh part of the time during which he has been on duty without interruption: Provided that no Privilege Leave can be earned by an Officer by duty done, while three months' such leave is due to him, and that, whenever duty is interrupted, all claim to Privilege Leave earned theretofore is forfeited. Absence on Privilege Leave, though not counting as duty, is not an interruption of duty within the meaning of this Section.

1. The calculation must be made as follows:—One calendar month for every eleven complete calendar months of duty, and one day for every eleven days of the balance.

4. "Examination Leave" may, if the Examination for which it is granted be successfully passed, or unless the Officer has already had twelve months' Examination Leave, count as duty qualifying for Privilege Leave. "Examination Leave" does not interrupt duty within the meaning of this Section, unless in the case of leave granted under Clauses (c) and (d) of Rule 1 in Section 76, the Officer fails to pass the Examination for which the Leave is granted.

72. The Privilege Leave due to an Officer is the Privilege Leave which he has earned, less the period during which he has been absent on Privilege Leave.

1. To Uncovenanted Judicial and Educational Officers whose duties are not continuous, and who enjoy regular vacations, during which they are allowed to be absent from duty, Privilege Leave is not admissible, except in case of urgent necessity. When Privilege Leave is granted under this rule, the officer taking it shall only receive one-half of the salary and allowances ordinarily admissible during Privilege Leave.

Note.—This rule does not apply to the case of an Uncovenanted Judicial officer who is prevented in any year from availing himself of the vacation or vacations by reason of his having to remain at his post on duty. In such a case Privilege Leave may be granted under the ordinary Rules.

* Applies to a Military Officer subject to Military Rules.

† Applies to all Officers, including Military Officers, subject to Military Rules.

* Applies to a Military Officer subject to Military Rules.

2. District and Sessions Judges who are prevented from availing themselves of the periodical court vacations by reason of having to hold sessions, or to hear criminal appeals, or to perform other official duties within the limits of their charges, during the vacations, may be allowed Privilege Leave under the ordinary rules.

To District and Sessions Judges, who are permitted to avail themselves of the periodical court vacations, Privilege Leave shall only be admissible on the terms stated in Rule 1.

3. The Privilege Leave admissible under Rules 1 and 2 may be accumulated under the ordinary Rules; but it cannot in any case be combined with vacation.

73. To an Officer who has been on duty, without interruption, for eleven calendar months, and who has not, for six calendar months, been absent on Privilege Leave, the whole or any part of the Privilege Leave due to him may be granted.

1. An Officer applying for Privilege Leave must record a declaration that he has no intention of retiring or of taking Furlough, Special Leave, Subsidiary Leave, Leave on Private Affairs or Leave on Medical Certificate for three months after his return to duty. Though not absolutely debarred by this declaration from applying for permission to retire or to take such leave within the three months, he should, if he does so, explain his change of mind.

74. An Officer on Privilege Leave has a lien on his substantive appointment; he has also a lien on his officiating appointment, so long as it is not resumed by an Officer having a superior lien on it.

75. An Officer on Privilege Leave is entitled to a Leave Allowance equal to the salary which he would receive if he were on duty in the appointment on which he has a lien.

1. If Privilege Leave is granted to an Officer who holds an appointment sanctioned only for a limited time and for a special purpose, he is not entitled, during Privilege Leave, to any Special Allowance attached to the appointment.

2. An officer on Privilege Leave is entitled to a Leave Allowance equal to his salary, even though another Officer be appointed to act for him.

3. An officer who has no lien on an appointment is entitled, during privilege leave—

In the case of a Covenanted Civil Servant, or a Military Officer subject to Civil Rules—to Subsistence Allowance.

In the case of an Uncovenanted Officer to no allowance.

EXAMINATION LEAVE.

[The Rules in Section 76 do not apply to Officers employed in the Public Works Department, in the Survey Department under the Surveyor General of India, or in the Forest Department: the Rules in this Chapter apply to Military Officers subject to Military Rules, serving in any other Civil Department.]

76. The following Rules regulate the grant of Leave to enable Officers to appear at Examinations:—

(1) (a) A Covenanted Civil Servant or a Military Officer subject to Civil Rules, who is a candidate for a reward, by the Higher Standard in Persian or Sanskrit, or by the High Proficiency Standard in the vernacular languages or Sanskrit, or the Burmese Language, or by the Degree of Honor Standard in the Vernacular languages or Sanskrit, is entitled to Joining Time to and from the place of examination, besides leave for the day or days of examination.

(b) A candidate for a reward by the Higher Standard in Arabic, or for High Proficiency in Arabic or Persian, may be allowed leave for one month, and if he passes the examination, for two months.

(c) The maximum Leave for two months authorised by Clause (b) may be prefixed to the examination if the candidate undertakes to spend it in study under professional tuition at a Presidency Town.

(d) An Officer, who is a candidate for the Degree of Honor in Arabic or Persian, may be allowed, instead of the leave for two months provided in Clause (c), leave for three months to Persia for Persian; and to Arabia, Mesopotamia, Egypt or Syria, for Arabic.

(2) An Officer on Leave under Rule 1 has a lien on his appointment, substantive or officiating, and is entitled to Leave Allowances, as if he were on Privilege Leave for an aggregate maximum period of twelve months.

(3) An officer while absent from his Office or from his Station to attend an obligatory Departmental Examination, or (in the Punjab) an examination in Pushtu or Biluchi, is considered to be on duty.

(4) Leave may not be given under Rule 3 to an Officer to prepare for examination, or for recreation after examination. A reasonable time, including the day or days of examination should be allowed for the journey to and from the place of examination, and nothing more.

(5) Leave may not be granted under this Section to a Military Officer subject to Military Rules to enable him to pass an examination under the orders of the Government of India in the Military Department, No. 1009, dated 3rd December, 1866.

EXTENSION AND COMMUTATION OF LEAVE.

77. Except as provided in Section 63, no kind of Leave can be granted in continuation of any other kind; but any Leave granted under the Rules in this Code may be, retrospectively, changed for any other kind or period of Leave which might, at first, have been granted:

Provided that the grant, or extension of Furlough, under this Rule (otherwise than on Medical Certificate), is subject to Proviso (3) under Section 49.

(1) When granting Furlough or Special Leave out of India, a Local Government should always state if there is any objection to its being prolonged or commuted under the Rules, and the Accountant-General should note any reservation of this kind upon the Absentee's Last Pay Certificate. When no such reservation is expressed by the Local Government, the Accountant General may always assume that none is intended. In the absence of such reservation, the Secretary of State will exercise jurisdiction under this Section in the case of an Officer on leave in Europe, without reference to the authorities in India: Provided always that an Officer on Furlough or Special Leave in Europe who wishes to have his Leave extended, must apply to the Secretary of State at least three months before the date on which his leave will expire in India; otherwise, he may be required to pay the cost of telegrams to and from India.

APPLICATIONS FOR LEAVE.

78. Every Application for Leave is to be submitted to the Local Government.

(1)* When a Gazetted Officer applies for Leave, he should quote the Section or Rule of this Code under which he considers himself entitled to the leave.

(15) If the Officer is going on Leave out of India, he should take with him one copy of the Medical Report upon his case.

(14)* A duplicate of the Medical Report upon the case of an Officer going to Europe on Medical Certificate should be forwarded through the Government under which the Officer is employed for the information of the Medical Board, attached to the India Office, so as to arrive as soon as the Officer arrives in England.

* Applies to all Officers.

† Applies to a Military officer subject to Military Rules.

(17) If an Applicant for an Extension or Commutation of Leave on Medical Certificate be residing out of India, and within sixty miles from London, he must produce a Certificate from the Medical Board attached to the India Office, showing the necessity for the Extension or Commutation. If he be residing more than sixty miles from London, Certificates must be produced from two Medical Practitioners, and he may be called upon to produce other evidence.

79. If under the operation of Proviso 3, Section

49, the applications for Furlough (including those under Section 77) cannot all be complied with. Furlough will be granted in the following order:—

First. To the Applicant to whom most Furlough is due:

Secondly. Of two or more Applicants to whom the same amount is due;—to him who has rendered longest Continuous Active Service:

Thirdly. Of two or more such Applicants who have rendered the same Continuous Active Service;—to the Senior.

CHAPTER VI.

NATIVE CIVIL SERVANTS.

80. During Leave on Medical Certificate in excess of 15 months at one time or of 30 months in all, and during Subsidiary Leave following such excess Leave, a Native Civil Servant is entitled to a quarter of his Average Salary. During any other Leave excepting Privilege Leave, Examination Leave or Extraordinary Leave, he is entitled to half his Average Salary:

Provided that his Leave Allowance shall not exceed his actual salary when he was last before on duty.

81. Leave on Medical Certificate may be granted for three years in all, but not for more than two years at one time.

82. A Native Civil Servant may take either Leave on Private Affairs or Furlough, as follows, but not both these kinds of Leave:—

(a) Leave on Private Affairs may be taken, first, after not less than six years' service, and, thereafter, at intervals of not less than six years. The duration of Leave on Private Affairs must not exceed six months at one time.

(b) Furlough, not exceeding two years in all, may be taken, first, after not less than ten years' service, and, thereafter, at intervals of not less than eight years. The duration of Furlough must not exceed one year at one time, unless it be taken, for the first time, after not less than eighteen years' service, when it may extend to two years.

83. Subsidiary Leave, Privilege Leave and Examination Leave may be granted to a Native Civil Servant under Sections 63—69, 70—75 and 76, respectively.

84. The Local Government may grant Extraordinary Leave without allowances at its discretion. Subject to the provisions of Section 88, there is no limit to the length or frequency of Leave under this Section; and it may be granted in continuation of any leave with allowances excepting Privilege Leave.

85. Leave taken by a Native Civil Servant before his appointment to an office in the Covenantal Civil Service, shall, for the purpose of calculating the Leave admissible to him under this Chapter, be treated as Leave taken under this Chapter.

86. Leave on Private Affairs or Furlough may not be granted in continuation of any Leave except Subsidiary Leave; but any Leave granted under these Rules may be retrospectively changed for any other kind or amount of Leave which might at first have been granted. If a Native Civil Servant absent on Privilege Leave, Leave on Private Affairs, or Furlough, takes extension of Leave on Medical Certificate, the whole of his absence must be treated as Leave on Medical Certificate.

1. Extraordinary Leave cannot be changed retrospectively into Leave on Medical Certificate; but Leave on Medical Certificate may be given in continuation of Extraordinary Leave.

87. A Native Civil Servant who has reached the age of fifty-five years, is not eligible for any Leave, excepting Privilege Leave. Leave, other than Privilege Leave, granted to a Native Civil Servant before his fifty-fifth birthday, cannot extend beyond that date.

88. If a Native Civil Servant is absent without leave, or remains absent after the end of Leave (excepting Privilege Leave, in which case a week's grace is allowed), he vacates his appointment; and, after five years' continuous absence from duty, whether with or without leave, a Native Civil Servant ceases to belong to the Public Service.

1. A Native Civil Servant who takes Leave other than Privilege Leave or Examination Leave, has no claim to return to the particular appointment from which he took Leave.

89. The procedure upon Application for Leave is that prescribed in Sections 73 and 79.

CHAPTER VII.LAW OFFICERS.Rules regarding Leave and Acting Allowances.

[Note.—In these Rules, the term “Law Officer” includes—

  • An Advocate General.
  • A Standing Counsel.
  • An Administrator General.
  • An Official Trustee.
  • An Official Assignee.
  • A Receiver of a High Court.
  • An Officer of a High Court who holds an appointment which, by law, can be held only by a Barrister.
  • Secretary in the Legislative Department to a Local Government.
  • A Remembrancer or Assistant Remembrancer of Legal Affairs.
  • A Government Advocate.
  • A Clerk of the Crown.
  • A Government Solicitor.
  • A Government Pleader, and
  • A Government Prosecutor.]

90. The Leave of Absence and Acting Allowances of a Law Officer who is a Covenanted Civil Servant are regulated by the provisions of the Civil Leave and Pay and Acting Allowance Codes.

91. The Leave of Absence and Acting Allowances of a Law Officer who is not a Covenanted Civil Servant, or a Government Pleader or a Government Prosecutor, but whose pay is fixed, and his whole time retained for the service of Government, are regulated by the Rules in Chapter V., and by the Pay and Acting Allowance Codes, as if he were a Covenanted Civil Servant.

92. A Government Pleader or Government Prosecutor is entitled to such Leave of Absence and Allowances when on Leave, and to such Acting Allowances, as the Authority who appoints him may think fit to grant; provided that no expense be caused to the Government.

93. The Leave of Absence and Acting Allowances of a Law Officer who is not a Covenanted Civil Servant or a Government Pleader or a Government Prosecutor, whose pay is fixed but whose whole time is not retained for the Public Service, are regulated by the following Rules:—

  • I. “Active Service” includes, besides Time spent on Duty, Leave of Absence taken under Rule III. and Subsidiary Leave of Absence taken under Rule VIII.
  • II. An Application for Leave of Absence must be submitted, through the Officer who passes his pay, to the Authority to which the Officer is directly subordinate.
  • III. Leave of Absence during the Vacation of the High or Chief Court within whose jurisdiction the Officer serves, may be granted without deduction of pay, provided that no expense be caused by the grant of such Leave.
  • IV. Leave may be granted on Medical Certificate for a period not exceeding one year; and Leave so granted may, on Medical Certificate, be extended to twenty months.
  • V. Leave of Absence on Medical Certificate may not be granted again until after three years’ Active Service.

VI. Leave of Absence on Private Affairs for a period not exceeding six months, may be granted once only to an Officer who has rendered six years’ Active Service.

VII. After four years’ Active Service, Furlough without Pay, but with retention of appointment, may be granted for a period not exceeding one year.

VIII. In very urgent cases Subsidiary Leave of absence may be granted under Sections 63 and 64. Provided that no expense be caused by the grant of such Leave.

IX. Excepting Subsidiary Leave, no kind of Leave may be granted in continuation of any other kind of Leave; but any Leave granted under these Rules may be retrospectively changed for any other kind or amount of Leave which might at first have been granted.

X. If an Officer, who has obtained Leave of Absence, makes over charge of his Office before noon, his Leave begins on, and includes, the day on which he makes over charge; otherwise, it begins on, and includes, the following day.

XI. If an Officer resumes charge of his Office after noon, his Leave of absence ends on, and includes, the day on which he resumes charge; otherwise, it ends on, and includes, the day before he resumes charge.

XII. An Officer, while on leave under Rules IV., V., or VI., is entitled to half-pay: Provided that his maximum Leave Allowance shall be, if paid in India, Rs. 833½ a month, and, if paid at the Home Treasury of the Government of India, £250 a quarter.

XIII. An Officer officiating for an Officer on Leave under these Rules is entitled to the pay of the appointment, less the fraction of it drawn by the Absentee, in addition to the same fraction of the pay of his own substantive office, if any. In calculating this fraction, the maximum Leave Allowance of £250 a quarter, payable from the Home Treasury of the Government of India, is held to be equal to Rs. 2,500.

Provisos:—

  • (1) The minimum salary of a Standing Counsel at Calcutta is Rs. 1,000 a month:
  • (2) The minimum salary of a Government Advocate at Lahore, Allahabad, Rangoon, or Moulmein is three-quarters of the pay of the appointment.

XIV. An Officer officiating for an Officer deputed to act in another appointment, or on special duty, is entitled to two-thirds of the pay of the appointment in which he officiates, plus one-third of the pay of his own substantive appointment, if any.

94. Leave of Absence may be granted under the Rules in Section 93 to a Law Officer, who is paid by Fees, whether his whole time be retained for the service of Government or not, on condition that he makes such arrangements for the performance of his duties as are satisfactory to the Authority by which he is appointed, and on condition that, in case of his obtaining Leave of Absence on Private Affairs, under Rule VI., the Officiating Officer shall receive the whole of the Fees attached to the Office.

CHAPTER VIII.BISHOPS.

[Note.—These Rules do not apply to the Bishops of Rangún and Lahore, who, in official status, are Senior Chaplains, and, as such, are subject to the Rules in Chapter IX.]

96. A Bishop may be allowed Leave of Absence either on Medical Certificate or on Furlough.

97. The amount of Leave of Absence on Medical Certificate admissible to a Bishop is limited to two years.

98. Subject to the limitation in Section 97, Leave of Absence may be granted to a Bishop upon Medical Certificate for a period not exceeding one year. Leave so granted may be extended upon Medical Certificate to any period not exceeding eighteen calendar months.

99. The amount of Furlough admissible to a Bishop at one time is limited to six calendar months.

100. The amount of Furlough earned by a Bishop is one-eleventh of the time during which he has been on duty without interruption: Provided that no additional Furlough can be earned by duty done while six months' Furlough is due, and that, when duty is interrupted, all Furlough earned theretofore is cancelled. Absence on Furlough, though not counting as duty, is not an interruption of duty within the meaning of this Rule.

101. The Furlough due to a Bishop is the Furlough which he has earned less the period during which he has been absent on Furlough.

102. To a Bishop who has been on duty without interruption for thirty-three calendar months, and who has not for twelve calendar months been absent on Furlough, the whole or any part of the Furlough due to him may be granted.

103. An Archdeacon or a Chaplain appointed to hold charge of a Diocese during the absence of the Bishop upon Furlough or upon Leave on Medical Certificate is entitled to an allowance of Rs. 500 a month in addition to the pay of his substantive Office.

104. A Bishop while absent upon Furlough or upon Leave on Medical Certificate is entitled to full pay less Rs. 500 a month, but not to any other allowances.

105. An Archdeacon or a Chaplain appointed to hold charge of a Diocese during the absence of the Bishop upon Leave on Medical Certificate is entitled to the Travelling and other similar Allowances admissible to the Bishop.

106. The allowances of the Bishop of Madras or Bombay when performing the functions of the Bishop of Calcutta during his absence on leave, are regulated by 5 & 6 Vic., cap. 119. In such case the Bishop of Madras or Bombay is entitled to an Allowance at the rate of Rs. 833\frac{1}{3} a month in addition to his salary as Bishop of Madras or Bombay, and the Bishop of Calcutta will receive during such absence the salary of his office less Rs. 833\frac{1}{3} a month.

107. A Bishop of Madras or Bombay exercising the episcopal Jurisdiction and Functions appertaining to the See of Calcutta during a vacancy of the See by the demise of the Bishop thereof for the time being, or otherwise, is entitled to the full pay fixed for the office, viz., Rs. 3,831. 6. 8 per mensem.

108. Leave under these Rules may be granted to the Metropolitan by the Governor-General in Council, and to the Bishops of Madras and Bombay by the Governments of those Presidencies, on the recommendation of the Metropolitan.

CHAPTER IX.CHAPLAINS.GENERAL.

109. No Leave other than Privilege Leave under Section 121 may be granted to a Chaplain who has completed twenty-five years' service (see Section 39, Civil Pension Code). Leave other than Privilege Leave granted to a Chaplain before his completion of twenty-five years' service ceases to have effect on such completion.

FURLOUGH.

110. The amount of Furlough admissible to a Chaplain is limited to six years. All the Rules in this Chapter are subject to this limitation.

Furlough and Leave with Allowances on Medical Certificate under former Rules are counted as Furlough under this Rule.

111. The amount of Furlough "earned" by a Chaplain is one-fourth of his active service, and three months in addition thereto.

112. The amount of Furlough "due" to a Chaplain is the amount which he has earned, diminished by—

  1. (1) The amount of Furlough which he has enjoyed under these or any former Rules.
  2. (2) The excess over one year in the amount of Leave with Allowances on Medical Certificate which he has enjoyed under former Rules.

113. (a) To a Chaplain who has rendered three years' Continuous Service, Furlough for not more than two years may be granted as follows:—

  1. 1. On Medical Certificate,—unconditionally.
  2. 2. Without Medical Certificate,—subject to these provisos—
  1. (1) That the Furlough be due to him:
  2. (2) That he have rendered seven years' Active Service:
  3. (3) That the whole number of Chaplains absent on Furlough and Special Leave do not exceed the limit appointed by the Government of India.

Note.—Except on Medical Certificate, or on very Urgent Private Affairs, Furlough or Special Leave may not be granted to a Church of England Chaplain employed in the Diocese of Calcutta, Lahore or Rangún respectively, if 15 per cent. of the whole number of such Chaplains are already absent on Furlough or Special Leave. The Bishop of Calcutta will report to the Government of India when the limit is reached.

(b) Furlough taken under this Section may, on Medical Certificate, be extended to not more than three years.

CHAPTER IX—continued.

(c) The Furlough of a Chaplain is strictly limited to a period of three years at one time, and cannot be extended even without Allowances. But the Secretary of State for India reserves to himself the power of allowing a Chaplain to remain in Europe beyond three years, should special and exceptional circumstances require it.

114. (a) To a Chaplain who has not rendered three years' Continuous Service, Furlough for not more than one year may be granted on Medical Certificate.

(b) Furlough granted under this section for less than one year may, on Medical Certificate, be extended to not more than one year.

115. A Chaplain on Furlough is entitled to allowances as follows:—

On ordinary Furlough. On other Furlough.
Archdeacon, and the Senior Chaplain of the Church of Scotland ... .. £600 a year £480 a year.
Senior Chaplains ... .. £500 " £384 "
Junior Chaplains ... .. £350 " £300 "

Explanation.—Ordinary Furlough includes—

  1. (1) The first two years of each separate period of Furlough under Section 113.
  2. (2) So much of Furlough under Section 114 as may be due, provided that the Chaplain have rendered six months' Continuous Active Service.

Note.—A Chaplain on Furlough does not forfeit his past Leave Allowance by resigning the service without returning to India.

116. The beginning and ending of a Chaplain's Furlough and Special Leave are regulated by the Rules in Chapter V.

SPECIAL LEAVE.

117. Special Leave on Urgent Private Affairs may be granted at any time, for not more than six months:

Provided that a Chaplain, who has had Special Leave, must render six years' Active Service before he can again have such Leave.

118. (a) For the first period of a Chaplain's Special Leave, he is entitled to the Leave Allowance admissible during Ordinary Furlough.

(b) In subsequent periods he is entitled to no Leave Allowance.

SUBSIDIARY LEAVE OF ABSENCE.

119. A Chaplain is entitled to Subsidiary Leave under the Rules in Chapter V.

120. A Chaplain on Subsidiary Leave is entitled to the same Allowances as during the Leave to which it is Subsidiary.

  1. 1. A Chaplain may draw Allowances as if he were on Privilege Leave for any part of his Subsidiary Leave preparatory to retiring from the Service or going on Furlough or Special Leave, for which, if he were not retiring from the Service or going on Furlough or on Special Leave, Privilege Leave would be admissible to him.

PRIVILEGE LEAVE.

121. Privilege Leave may be granted as follows:—

After five months' uninterrupted duty,—for not more than one month.

After ten months' uninterrupted duty,—for not more than two months.

After fifteen months' uninterrupted duty,—for not more than three months.

  1. 1. In applying for Privilege Leave a Chaplain must record a declaration that he has no intention of retiring or of taking Furlough, Special Leave, Subsidiary Leave or Leave on Medical Certificate for three months after his return to duty. Though not absolutely debarred by this declaration from applying to retire or to take such leave within three months, he should, if he does so, explain his change of mind.
  2. 2. A Chaplain may not take Privilege Leave in instalments.

122. A Chaplain on Privilege Leave is entitled to the same pay and house-rent as if he were on duty.

MISCELLANEOUS.

123. The Rules for—

  1. (1) Extension and commutation of leave; and
  2. (2) Payment of leave allowances;

are those contained in Sections 77 and Chapter XIV, respectively. But to a Chaplain going on Furlough to England, an advance of the first quarter's allowances may be made, which advance is not recoverable in the event of his death.

CHAPTER X.

UNCOVENANTED OFFICERS GENERALLY.

[Except where they are explanatory of points in which the provisions of Chapter V differ from those in this Chapter, the Rules in Chapter V which are not expressly restricted to Covenanted Civil Servants, Military Officers, or Uncovenanted Officers subject to the Rules in that Chapter, apply to Officers subject to the Rules in this Chapter.]

EXTENT OF APPLICATION.

125. The following Rules apply to Officers who have Substantive Appointments on permanent establishments under the Government, and who are not entitled to Leave under the Rules in Chapters III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII and IX.

126. These Rules apply fully only to those whose pay is not less than Rs. 100. But Leave may be granted under them to an Officer, whether he be in Superior or Inferior Service, whose pay is less than Rs. 100, so far

as it can be done without imposing any cost upon the State. The Absentee Allowance of the substantive incumbent must not exceed what remains from the pay of the appointment after provision is made for the efficient discharge of his duties during his absence. No Leave in excess of the Leave admissible under these Rules may be granted to such an Officer, with allowances, or counting as service for pension.

LEAVE ON MEDICAL CERTIFICATE.

127. Leave on Medical Certificate may be granted

CHAPTER X—continued.

for three years in all, but not for more than two years at one time; and no Officer can have Leave on Medical Certificate out of India more than twice.

128. An Officer on Leave under Section 127 is entitled to Half his Average Salary for the first fifteen months of each period of absence, but not for more than thirty months in all. For the rest of his leave under Section 127 he is entitled to a Quarter of his Average Salary.

LEAVE ON PRIVATE AFFAIRS.

129. Leave on Private Affairs for six months may be granted to an Officer who has not had Furlough, after six years' service, and repeated after intervals of six years.

  1. (1) Leave on Private Affairs does not accumulate, and cannot be taken in instalments.
  2. (2) Leave on Medical Certificate counts as service for Leave on Private Affairs.

130. An Officer on Leave under Section 129 is entitled to Half his Average Salary.

  1. (1) An Officer does not forfeit the allowance to which he is entitled under this Section by resigning the Service at the end of the Leave.
FURLOUGH.

131. Furlough may be granted as follows to an Officer who has not had Leave on Private Affairs:—
